summary: Broad near-match cleanup across 24 files, primarily correcting data-value-sensitive mismatches: wrong called functions, wrong float/data constants, wrong helper/prototype signatures, and a few fake-match artifacts. The PR author explicitly attributed many issues to code that was nearly 100% matched but had incorrect data values such as bad `bl` targets or float literals, and noted using a script to find functions whose remaining diffs were only wrong branch-link instructions.
what_changed: Corrected many near-match call targets, especially in Kirby special move code. Examples in the excerpt include `it_802AEAB4` changed to `it_802ADC34`, `ftAnim_8006EBA4` changed to `ftKb_SpecialN_800F9070` in eat-state entry paths, and collision helpers switching between `ft_80082708`, `ft_800827A0`, and `ft_80081D0C` depending on the expected ground/air transition behavior.

Replaced hardcoded float literals in several `Fighter_ChangeMotionState` calls with external data constants where the original code referenced data symbols, e.g. `ftKb_Init_804D9368`, `ftKb_Init_804D936C`, `ftKb_Init_804D93E8`, `ftKb_Init_804D93EC`, `ftKb_Init_804D94F0`, `ftKb_Init_804D94F4`, `ftKb_Init_804D9478`, and `ftKb_Init_804D9480`. In another case, Yoshi-copy momentum `vec->z` changed from `0.0f` to `ftKb_Init_804D9558`.

Fixed a local declaration/prototype mismatch by replacing `ftAnim_80070458_proto(Fighter*, CostumeTObjList*, int)` with the real-looking `ftAnim_80070458(Fighter*, CostumeTObjList*, u32 tobj_idx, float frame)` and updated the call to pass both `arg1` and `arg2`.

Updated `ftKb_SpecialNYs_8010AC78` declaration in `ftKb_Init.h` from one argument to two arguments, naming them `victim` and `gobj`, and adjusted Yoshi-copy capture animation code to call `ftKb_SpecialNYs_8010AC78(victim, gobj)`.

Implemented previously placeholder-documented Kirby Yoshi-copy animation functions in `ftKb_SpecialNYs.c`, including `ftKb_YsSpecialNCapture2_1_Anim` and `ftKb_YsSpecialAirN2_0_Anim`, with repeated hat animation, command-variable handling, victim callbacks, and end-of-animation transition to either `ft_8008A2BC` or `ftCo_Fall_Enter`.
Removed fake-match/regalloc scaffolding in `fn_801098EC`, replacing a fabricated comma-operator/null variable path with a direct `NULL` argument to `Fighter_ChangeMotionState`.

Changed `ftKb_MsSpecialAirNLoop_Anim` behavior from calling `ftAnim_IsFramesRemaining(gobj)` after setting `cmd_vars[0]` to calling `ftKb_SpecialNPe_8010C1E8(gobj)`, indicating a wrong call target was hiding in a near-match.

Touched non-Kirby areas as part of the same data-value cleanup: game mode result files, Big Blue and Venom stage files, several item files, toy code, and sysdolphin baselib particle/hsd code. The excerpt does not show their exact hunks, so details are weaker for those files.
smart_moves: The author treated near-matches as suspicious even when structural code looked correct, focusing on data values such as `bl` targets and float/data operands rather than only instruction shape.
A script was used to find functions whose remaining diffs were only wrong `bl` instructions, turning a broad manual review problem into a searchable mechanical cleanup pass.

The PR reused actual data symbols for float constants instead of forcing equivalent-looking literals. This matters because relocations/data references can differ even when runtime numeric values look like `0.0f` or `1.0f`.
The fix removed fake-match code once the true expression was identified, improving maintainability while preserving or improving matching.
Prototype/header correction was paired with call-site correction, preventing a local fake declaration from masking a real function signature and argument-passing mismatch.
decomp_lessons: Near-match status is not sufficient if the diffing setup ignores data values; wrong call targets and wrong float relocations can survive as nearly matched functions.
Use `m2c` or equivalent disassembly-informed output as a starting point when possible; the author suspected some bad near-matches came from agents that did not use `m2c`.

When diffing decomp output, use settings that expose relocation/data-value differences. The author specifically mentioned `functionRelocDiffs=data_value` as a likely missing setting behind these errors.
Do not replace referenced float constants with literal `0.0f` or `1.0f` just because the numeric value appears equivalent. Matching may require the exact data symbol and relocation.

Wrong helper calls can be visually plausible because related functions have similar names and signatures, e.g. ground/air collision helpers and item cleanup helpers. Verify the exact branch target against asm rather than relying on semantic guesswork.
Fake regalloc constructs should be revisited after a better match is found. A `NULL` argument is preferable to fabricated local variables/comma-operator tricks when it matches.
Headers and prototypes should reflect the true call ABI. A fabricated proto with missing parameters can allow C to compile but will hide the actual decomp mismatch.
naming_conventions: External float data symbols in Kirby init code are kept in address-style names such as `ftKb_Init_804D9368`, `ftKb_Init_804D936C`, `ftKb_Init_804D93E8`, and similar, rather than inventing semantic names without evidence.
Function names retain existing decomp address-style names where semantics are not known, e.g. `fn_801098EC`, `ftKb_SpecialN_800F9070`, and `ftKb_SpecialNPe_8010C1E8`.
When a function signature was clarified, parameter names used semantic roles where evident from call sites: `ftKb_SpecialNYs_8010AC78(Fighter_GObj* victim, Fighter_GObj* gobj)`.
The corrected `ftAnim_80070458` prototype names the third and fourth parameters `tobj_idx` and `frame`, providing more useful documentation than the old placeholder `ftAnim_80070458_proto`.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Search for functions whose only remaining differences are wrong `bl` instructions; these are likely wrong call targets rather than structural C issues.
Enable or use diff tooling that reports relocation/data-value mismatches, specifically the `functionRelocDiffs=data_value` mode mentioned by the author.
Audit near-matches for wrong float literal encoding versus external data references; matching may require `extern f32` or `extern const f32` declarations and passing those symbols into calls.

For motion-state calls, verify every argument including flags, start frame, anim rate, blend/frame arguments, and final pointer argument. Many changes were one-argument or one-symbol corrections in otherwise plausible calls.

For collision transition functions, compare exact helper call targets (`ft_80082708`, `ft_800827A0`, `ft_80081D0C`) and corresponding common-state helpers (`ftCommon_8007D5D4` versus `ftCommon_8007D7FC`) against asm.
Remove temporary fake-match variables when the true call argument is simply `NULL`; do not keep fake comma-operator/regalloc workarounds unless the asm still requires them.
review_feedback: No inline review comments were present in the provided slice.

A PR comment asked whether match reporting ignores data values, noting that there were many more fixes than the bot reported. This reinforces that bot/match reporting may undercount relocation/data-value-only corrections.

The PR body states uncertainty about how the errors were introduced, but hypothesizes they came from agents not using `m2c` as a starting point or not using `functionRelocDiffs=data_value` when diffing.
follow_up_queries: Find other near-matched functions with data-value-only relocation diffs, especially wrong `bl` targets.
Audit recent agent-authored matches for missing `m2c` baseline or diffing without `functionRelocDiffs=data_value`.
Search for local `_proto` function declarations that differ from real signatures and may hide wrong argument counts.
Search for `Fighter_ChangeMotionState` calls using literal `0.0f, 1.0f, 0.0f` where asm may reference external float data symbols.
Search for remaining fake-match comments, comma-operator hacks, or fabricated locals that can be removed after correcting call targets/data symbols.
Check whether match reporting/bot output accounts for data-value-only fixes and wrong branch targets.
